title_en: Specific Shafi'i Maxims and Legal Parallels
title_id: Kaidah-kaidah Syafi'i Khusus dan Paralel-paralel Hukum
content_id:
Di luar lima kaidah universal, Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir menyajikan ratusan kaidah khusus untuk mazhab Syafi'i dan contoh-contoh kasus paralel (naza'ir) di mana prinsip yang sama menghasilkan keputusan yang sama dalam konteks hukum yang berbeda. Bagian kedua dari karya ini sama berharganya dengan yang pertama bagi penuntut ilmu Syafi'i tingkat lanjut.

Sebuah kaidah Syafi'i yang menonjol adalah: 'Dalam ibadah, aturan asalnya adalah larangan' (al-ashl fi al-'ibadat al-tahrim). Ini kontras dengan kebolehan asal dalam muamalat. Implikasi praktisnya adalah bahwa seseorang tidak boleh menciptakan bentuk-bentuk ibadah wajib atau sunnah yang baru tanpa dasar tekstual. Ini adalah akar dari penolakan mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali terhadap bentuk-bentuk tertentu dari perayaan atau praktik keagamaan yang tidak memiliki preseden kenabian yang jelas — prinsip yang dikenal sebagai penolakan bid'ah (inovasi dalam praktik keagamaan).

Sebuah kaidah Syafi'i yang terkenal dengan aplikasi yang luas adalah: 'Keyakinan tidak dihilangkan oleh keraguan' (al-yaqin la yazul bi al-syakk), yang dibahas secara luas dalam bab kaidah universal tetapi diberikan aplikasi khusus Syafi'i di sini. Mazhab Syafi'i menerapkan kaidah ini dengan konsistensi yang khusus — dalam pertanyaan tentang menyentuh perempuan, karena mazhab Syafi'i berpendapat bahwa menyentuh membatalkan wudhu, jika seorang pria tidak yakin apakah ia menyentuh istrinya atau tidak, wudhunya tetap sah karena keyakinan (keadaan sebelumnya memiliki wudhu) tidak dihilangkan oleh keraguan.

Bagian naza'ir (paralel-paralel) mendemonstrasikan kekuatan penalaran yang konsisten di berbagai domain hukum. Satu set paralel yang klasik: dalam shalat, menambahkan rakaat kelima dengan sengaja membatalkan shalat; dalam haji, dengan sengaja menambahkan satu putaran tawaf (syawth) melebihi tujuh yang diwajibkan tidak membatalkan tawaf; dalam puasa, dengan sengaja memakan sesuatu yang sangat sedikit (di bawah apa yang biasanya merupakan makan) membatalkan puasa; dalam zakat, membayar sedikit lebih dari jumlah yang diwajibkan adalah sah tanpa menjadi wajib. Al-Suyuthi menganalisis mengapa tindakan umum yang sama — melebihi kuantitas yang ditentukan — memiliki konsekuensi hukum yang berbeda di setiap konteks, mengungkapkan prinsip-prinsip penalaran yang mengatur setiap domain.

Perlakuan al-Suyuthi atas pengecualian-pengecualian terhadap kaidah-kaidah sangat berharga. Setiap kaidah hukum memiliki pengecualian — situasi di mana prinsip yang mengatur tidak berlaku. Untuk kaidah 'Keyakinan tidak dihilangkan oleh keraguan,' sebuah pengecualian besar adalah hukum pembuktian: setelah pengadilan menetapkan suatu fakta melalui kesaksian yang sah, keraguan berikutnya tentang kesaksian tersebut tidak membatalkan keputusan. Keputusan pengadilan, sekali final, mengambil karakter kepastian hukum yang menolak keraguan berikutnya. Al-Suyuthi dengan cermat memetakan pengecualian-pengecualian ini, mencegah penuntut ilmu menerapkan kaidah mana pun secara berlebihan.

Karya ini diakhiri dengan refleksi al-Suyuthi tentang tujuan menguasai kaidah-kaidah hukum. Yuris yang mengetahui keputusan-keputusan spesifik tanpa memahami prinsip-prinsip yang mendasarinya adalah seperti dokter yang mengetahui resep-resep tanpa memahami farmakologi — mampu dalam kasus-kasus rutin tetapi tersesat ketika menghadapi hal yang baru. Ulama yang memahami kaidah-kaidah dapat menurunkan keputusan-keputusan untuk situasi-situasi baru dengan penuh keyakinan, mengetahui bahwa penalaran mereka didasarkan pada prinsip-prinsip yang sama yang menghasilkan keputusan-keputusan klasik yang telah mereka pelajari. Kompetensi intelektual ini — kemampuan untuk bernalar secara sistematis dari prinsip-prinsip, bukan sekadar mengingat keputusan-keputusan yang dihafal — adalah tujuan pendidikan hukum Islam tingkat lanjut.
content_en:
Beyond the five universal maxims, Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir presents hundreds of maxims specific to the Shafi'i school and examples of parallel cases (naza'ir) in which the same principle generates the same ruling in different legal contexts. This second part of the work is as valuable as the first for the advanced Shafi'i student.

A prominent Shafi'i maxim is: 'In acts of worship, the original rule is prohibition' (al-asl fi-l-'ibadat at-tahrim). This contrasts with the original permissibility in transactions. The practical implication is that one may not innovate new forms of obligatory or recommended worship without textual basis. This is the root of the Shafi'i and Hanbali opposition to certain forms of religious celebration or practice that lack clear prophetic precedent — the principle known as the rejection of bid'ah (innovation in religious practice).

A celebrated Shafi'i maxim with wide applications is: 'Certainty is not removed by doubt' (al-yaqin la yazul bish-shakk), treated extensively in the universal maxims chapter but with Shafi'i-specific applications given here. The Shafi'i school applies this maxim with particular consistency — in the question of touching a woman, since the Shafi'i school holds that touching nullifies wudu, if a man is uncertain whether he touched his wife or not, his wudu remains valid because certainty (the prior state of having wudu) is not removed by doubt.

The naza'ir (parallels) section demonstrates the power of consistent reasoning across different legal domains. A classic set of parallels: in prayer, adding a fifth rak'ah intentionally invalidates the prayer; in hajj, intentionally adding a tawaf circuit (shawt) beyond the required seven does not invalidate the tawaf; in fasting, intentionally eating a tiny amount (below what normally constitutes eating) breaks the fast; in zakah, paying slightly more than the obligatory amount is valid without being obligatory. As-Suyuti analyzes why the same general act — exceeding the specified quantity — has different legal consequences in each context, revealing the reasoning principles that govern each domain.

As-Suyuti's treatment of exceptions to maxims is particularly valuable. Every legal maxim has exceptions — situations where the governing principle does not apply. For the maxim 'Certainty is not removed by doubt,' a major exception is the law of evidence: once a court has established a fact through valid testimony, a subsequent doubt about that testimony does not nullify the judgment. The court's judgment, once final, takes on the character of legal certainty that resists subsequent doubt. As-Suyuti carefully maps these exceptions, preventing the student from over-applying any maxim.

The work concludes with as-Suyuti's reflection on the purpose of mastering legal maxims. The jurist who knows specific rulings without understanding their underlying principles is like a physician who knows prescriptions without understanding pharmacology — capable in routine cases but lost when facing anything novel. The scholar who understands the maxims can derive rulings for new situations with confidence, knowing that their reasoning is grounded in the same principles that generated the classical rulings they have studied. This intellectual competence — the ability to reason systematically from principles, not just to recall memorized rulings — is the goal of advanced Islamic legal education.
content_ar: Beyond the five universal maxims, Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir presents hundreds of maxims specific to the الشافعي school and examples of parallel cases (naza'ir) in which the same principle generates the same ruling in different legal contexts. This ثانياً part of the work is as valuable as the أولاً for the advanced الشافعي student. A prominent الشافعي maxim is: 'In acts of worship, the original rule is prohibition' (al-asl fi-l-'العبادة at-tahrim). This contrasts with the original permissibility in transactions. The practical implication is that one may not innovate new forms of obligatory or recommended worship without textual basis. This is the root of the الشافعي and الحنبلي opposition to certain forms of religious celebration or practice that lack clear prophetic precedent — the principle known as the rejection of البدعة (البدعة in religious practice). A celebrated الشافعي maxim with wide applications is: 'Certainty is not removed by doubt' (al-yaqin la yazul bish-shakk), treated extensively in the universal maxims الباب but with الشافعي-specific applications given here. The الشافعي school applies this maxim with particular consistency — in the question of touching a woman, بما أن the الشافعي school holds that touching nullifies الوضوء, إذا a man is uncertain whether he touched his الزوجة or not, his الوضوء remains valid لأن certainty (the prior state of having الوضوء) is not removed by doubt. The naza'ir (parallels) الفصل demonstrates the power of consistent reasoning across different legal domains. A classic set of parallels: in الصلاة, adding a خامساً rak'ah intentionally invalidates the الصلاة; in الحج, intentionally adding a tawaf circuit (shawt) beyond the required seven does not invalidate the tawaf; in الصيام, intentionally eating a tiny amount (below what normally constitutes eating) breaks the fast; in الزكاة, paying slightly more than the obligatory amount is valid without being obligatory. As-Suyuti analyzes why the same general act — exceeding the specified quantity — has different legal consequences in each context, revealing the reasoning principles that govern each domain. As-Suyuti's treatment of exceptions to maxims is particularly valuable. Every legal maxim has exceptions — situations where the governing principle does not apply. For the maxim 'Certainty is not removed by doubt,' a major exception is the law of evidence: حين a court has established a fact through valid testimony, a subsequent doubt about that testimony does not nullify the judgment. The court's judgment, حين final, takes on the الأخلاق of legal certainty that resists subsequent doubt. As-Suyuti carefully maps these exceptions, preventing the student from over-applying any maxim. The work concludes with as-Suyuti's reflection on the purpose of mastering legal maxims.
